speaker: interviewer
text: Tape 4, interview with Vera Ray. You were talking about coming to Romania. On the way, what did you observe? Did you see any destruction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I will answer this in a roundabout way. I can't remember where I saw the destruction exactly. I remember seeing a lot of destruction. I remember seeing a lot of ruins. I remember seeing a lot of displaced people. And I remember having that feeling of being close to death a lot of the time. A lot of that, it, it-- I remember, you know, that, that death was with me, with me all the time, you know. I-- you know, people were dying. And I saw people-- you know, as a kid, you, you-- to see death at such close quarters, it, it was all very confusing. But I can't tell you whether, whether I saw it from Romania, you know. What I actually remember of that, of that trip was we, we had a day in Moscow, and I had never seen a metro with all those beautiful statues. That's what stayed in my mind-- and having a fight with a boy called Otto because he pinched my stamps.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you know, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there's a, there's a general feeling of danger. Like we had to learn how to use a rifle at school. We had compulsory rifle practice. I mean, you know, you never forgot that there was a war on. You never forgot that, that you, you, could-- a lot of the times, I could hear the war. You know, it was a few kilometers up the road.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- we lived in a war. But I can't tell you whether I saw it on the way to-- from one place to another. That, I can't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what do you-- when you came to Romania, where did you finish there in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's when I really decided I had enough. Another language, another school-- I just jacked up, and I wouldn't go. But I went anyway. But I wouldn't-- I didn't do any work. I just-- I remember sitting at these high desks and thinking, I'm not doing it. Enough. I wasn't going to learn Romanian. That was just too much.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my rebelliousness of my childhood made me-- my, my poor father, you know? He didn't know what to do with me. I just jacked up. And, and, and so I was again a naughty girl. But I'd had enough. So I sat at school and did nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I remember sitting at this desk. How I managed to do nothing-- it must have been so boring. But I was just full of anger. I was getting, you know, on the verge of teenagehood. And I don't know what my hormones were doing, but I just was full of, hey, I've had enough of this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall where were you accommodated? How were you accommodated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a very large room. And I remember there must have been, I don't know, a dozen girls in that room. And we had the beds all around the room. And I was the second-- well, was I the second oldest? I was not. There were two or three. But I was-- I wasn't the youngest, for sure. And I became the ringleader of every, every naughtiness. I remember we escaped through the windows one night and went to the pictures. And so my father just took me out of there. And he made me stay in his quarters, you know, for punishment. But I thought it was quite nice to, you know, to actually stay with my dad, not with the other kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was a handful. It was just, let me out of here. Where's my mother? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't see her till the end of the war, till we got to Slovakia. Did you have any contact with her through letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't remember that. It doesn't mean that I didn't, but I certainly-- I-- you know, I'm inclined to say no. Because she was still in the front. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But then, there is the evidence of that one letter which she got with, with my tear-stained letter, so there must have been something going through. But I don't have a feeling that I was in a lot of contact with her. I don't remember any contact except for this one thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which place in Romania was the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bucharest. It was the first time since I was six years old my father took me to a cake shop. And he showed me all these cakes. And my father told me. I can't remember this. He said that I stood there mesmerized, absolutely mesmerized. He said I could choose one piece. And that was torture. I wanted everything. I haven't changed. [LAUGHS].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that was really, you know, going back to-- normal, I was going to say, but I really can't remember that kind of normal life when I was six. So yeah, it was amazing. It was like, this is how people live? And how did the local population relate to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think we had much to do with them. You know, I went to school and sat, and then I went home. And I had my own kids to play with. So I think we were, you know, isolated from them. And we knew that the war-- there was this other feeling. The war is finishing, you know. And I, I think that also, I didn't get stuck into school, because there was this feeling of, hey, this is, this is just temporary. And you know, that feeling that the war was finishing-- not that I believed it. I really-- after six years, there wasn't anything that one really believed. But one hoped. So hope started to grow, not, not, not belief. It was like, maybe it's gonna finish one day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you keep your hope before?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have any. There was nothing to keep. It was just survival. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I never hoped. I didn't know what, what, what would I be hoping for? I couldn't remember anything much. I certainly did not hope that-- no, it was-- the hope would have been useless, really. Hope would have gotten in the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And kids, kids just survive. What am I going to do now? What am I going to eat now? How am I going to avoid this big bully now? Where are we gonna get some wood so we can keep warm? We used to have stealing expeditions to get wood. You know, it was survival. Do you recall any useful thoughts or, or things that, that kept you surviving?

speaker: interviewee
text: These books that I read, you know, they were a real antidote. Because I-- you know, Byron and Lermontov, they were all-- this is what I-- this is how I heard, heard the thing. It was like, you can stand up and say no. It was a real antidote to what my mother was saying, was hide. But I never really saw it like that. All I know-- all I knew was that I-- I remember, you know, I used to collect-- I used to write down-- every time I read a book, I wrote it down. And by the age of 13, I had 1,000 titles. So I didn't start reading until I was eight.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm not-- I'm still not good at math. It's all those schools, I say. It's a lot of books in, in, in one's short lifespan. I was looking for answers. How did you access those books?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, that was one good thing about Russia. They had libraries. And the libraries were divided by age group. And I remember in Buzuluk and Yefremov, I, I just read everything that was there for my age group. And I remember I talked the librarian into letting me read adult books. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were adult books. They were Dostoevsky. They were Tolstoy. They were-- they were adult books. How I talked her into it, I don't know. But I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: So there were always libraries. I remember libraries in Yefremov. I remember libraries in Buzuluk. Before that, I don't know that I, that I could read. I mean, I was eight, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] nine.

speaker: interviewee
text: But by the time I got to Buzuluk, I was 10, and I was reading everything that wasn't nailed down in the middle of the night. I really wanted to know something. I didn't know what I wanted to know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where there any adults that you could talk about those books?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never talked to anyone. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Actually, I never talked to anyone, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] no.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Bucharest, how did you find out that the war was over?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess my dad told me. There was the- there would have been news, of course. The news would have been after Stalingrad. You know, the, the Germans were, were fleeing. And that would have been blaring from all the loudspeakers all the time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That wouldn't have-- that news wouldn't have been hard to acquire, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I didn't believe anything until I saw my mother. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It didn't kind of matter. You know, we moved again. I was in another country again. This time, it was a Slovak school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you move?

speaker: interviewee
text: Don't know. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No idea. I just remember Kosice, Kosice, standing in front of a door. Ringing the doorbell. My mother opening the door and saying, [NON-ENGLISH]. What are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought, well, couldn't you just say something else? It was such a big disappointment. But she just got a shock of her life. How did you get here? Never mind how I got here. I'm here. So I, I, I did say to her that I was really disappointed about how she greeted me and-- but what-- first thing she expressed was shock. Poor old mom. She-- you know, I was so much trouble. She'd have been better off with three other kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was Kosice. We stayed in Kosice for-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 13. I was 13. No, I wasn't-- 12. So we must have been there for a year. And again, we stayed in a house of Jewish people-- no. He was Jewish. She was not Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they came out. We actually got them out to come to Australia. And-- but they only spoke, spoke Hungarian. Now, Hungarian, I couldn't learn. You know, I really jacked up. Romanian and Hungarian are very difficult languages. So-- but we didn't stay there very long, and we ended up in Prague. And that's where I thought my life would begin to normalize. And two years later, we were in Australia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- when were you in Kosice, how-- the people that, that lived there through the war, how did they relate to you after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think they wanted us back. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] I didn't feel that we were welcome. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't feel good at school. But it could have been because, you know, why should I feel good yet again? [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] It was very, very strange. Suddenly, the war was over. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Kosice is not very clear to me. It's not a very clear. Because we weren't there very long. We would have been there for at least half a year, because I remember going to school. Maybe we were there for a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what do I remember? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All I know is that my parents didn't feel at all settled or welcome. And they just couldn't get out fast enough. Did you go back to Orlova?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, and I don't know why. I wish I'd have asked my parents before they died, why we never went back to Orlova. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father went to [NON-ENGLISH] for a short while. He had a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's not that he had a shop, but he was again put in charge of something to start it up. And my mother had a job in the Ministry of Agriculture. And we got this flat in Prague. Now, there was a funny word. You know, the, the, the Germans, when they took Jewish property, in Czech, we said, [NON-ENGLISH]. That means they made it Aryan. Well, we came back and we [NON-ENGLISH]. So we took away their property. And we had this really nice little flat which belonged to a German professor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was, there was a little bit of gladness, you know, that OK, the tables are now turned. But it was very weird, you know? My-- I mean, my parents spoke German, Czech-German. But German was a language that was-- I grew up with. And Jews got into trouble for speaking German on the streets. So German was out. We, we came to-- my parents-- my mother was particularly paranoid about being Jewish. We're not Jewish. So we were-- I was stuck with I'm not Jewish, and what does, what do you put on a piece of paper? [NON-ENGLISH]. That means without denomination.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, I had a girlfriend whom I met 40 years later. And she said to me, I knew you were Jewish. And oh, god. If I-- I mean, I'd like to wipe that past of my life out, because I am so ashamed that we couldn't find a way of, of being proud Jews rather than ashamed Jews. It's, you know, internalized anti-Semitism. That upset me a lot. Anyway, I'm not doing it anymore. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you lived in, in Prague, your father lived away then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just for a while-- just till he, till he set up this business. Then he came back to Prague. We didn't stay in Prague. We only stayed in Prague two years. But those two years were the most significant two years of my life. Because I started to believe that the war was over. And I started to put down roots. And I started to be a teenager. And I started to fall in love. And I started to make plans about my future.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next thing I know, my father's saying, we're moving again. Why are we moving? My mother had a fit. Why are we moving now? Because the communists are coming. Did you like Russia? No, we didn't like Russia. All right, we're going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they nearly got divorced then, because Mom said, I'm not going. And he said, OK, you stay. I'm taking the child. That was me. And she said, all right, I'm going.

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, you know? He-- I don't know what he had-- a sixth sense. He got us out in time '48. It was just before the Gottwald takeover, the communist takeover. Before then, how-- the soldiers from the Czech army and Soviet Union, how were they treated after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going to say as heroes. It's probably right-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] probably right. Well, they didn't rape anyone, like the Russians did. And, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they, they were the, you know, they were the-- what do you call them when they, when they free somebody? Liberators. They were the liberators. But they didn't do the things that the Russians did. I mean, the Russians were just as bad as the Germans, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So in spite of the respect that your father was receiving, he, he felt that it's not going to last?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was just from the Czech population. But he knew what communism meant. He was the most virulent anti-communist. He was really-- I mean, my mother kept a little bit of, you know-- Well, when we came, when we came to Australia, he voted for the DLP. Now, you probably don't know what DLP means, but they were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, I don't want to say they were fascists. They weren't fascist. But god, you know, Jews don't vote for the DLP, for Christ's sake. Christ's sake-- what a nice way to put it. My mother was a little bit more democrat-socialist, you know. I think she was just better educated than she thought. My, my father just went, nah, whatever-- whoever is against communists, I'm going to join them regardless. Because he had such a bad time in Russia. And, and he, he just-- you know. He wasn't a nice, liberal, middle-class man. He was-- he came from a poor background, and he called a spade a spade. And he, you know-- black was black, and white was white. And we had arguments. I had arguments with him because I didn't subscribe to his-- as I got a bit older-- to his ways. But we had a terrible fear of the Russians and of communism, just as much as of the Nazis. They could have been twin brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when your, when your father said that you're leaving again--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: --were you agreeing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was terrible. That was just terrible for me. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Oh, if I, if I would have been an adult, I wouldn't have gone. I wouldn't have gone. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because it was very hard when I came-- when we ended up in Prague, you know? I couldn't speak Czech. I couldn't write Czech. There were words in Czech. I mean, I spoke-- for six years, I spoke in Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Words-- like I remember, I had to give a speech-- it was just so ridiculous-- a speech in Czech. And I used-- there is a word, zivot. Now, in Russian, it means stomach. In, in Czech, it means life. I got those two confused. I can't remember exactly, but you can imagine me giving a-- I was giving a speech about life, and I ended up talking about my stomach because that's what I thought that was supposed to be. It was very embarrassing for a teenager, you know. And, and I got through all of that. It took me two years to, to get the feeling of, I belong here. And-- so when I came to Australia, I was a very angry teenager. Oh, boy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A very angry teenager-- very lonely, very depressed. I went to see a-- I made a couple of Jewish girlfriends. And I went to see a rabbi because I wanted-- I was very anti-God. And I said, said to the rabbi, [LAUGHS] you prove to me there is a God. And he said, here, take these books and read them. I said, no, I've read enough books. You prove to me. And he said, sorry. And that was the last time I spoke to a rabbi except for when I got married. We were married in a, in a synagogue. But yeah, so there were no answers. I didn't even have the questions. I just had fury. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So yeah, that's how that went. I liked Prague. And I really identified Prague as my home. And I knew that Prague was my home and that, you know, after 40 years, 30 years, in Australia, I didn't really know that I had any more feelings for over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then in '68 when, when Dubcek tried to change things, and we were watching things on television or whatever, and I got so upset. And I went to all these meetings and demonstrations. That's how I realized that I was still Czech-- Czech, Jewish, Australian, Russian, whatever. [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you choose Australia, or how did your father chose Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, my aunt and uncle were here. They got out in '39 or '38-- or they were here forever. They were here, so that's how we, we got the visa, or I don't know-- something that's necessary.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get the permission to leave Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was nothing to stop us. It was still all normal. The, the, the communists weren't, weren't in it yet. We just said, we want to go. So they said, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get here?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a story in itself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We got to-- we-- there was a little ship. We caught a train to Italy. There was a little ship from Italy to Egypt. And in Egypt, we got stuck. In Egypt there was a-- in the desert, there was a, like, a little village made of displaced persons. And there were all these tents. There was a man who came out with a great big beard, Czech. And my father said-- oh, you know, he said, oh, you know, you can have that tent. And then my father-- oh, well, we're not staying. We're just waiting for the, for our boat. And he said, he said-- he laughed. And he said, that's what I said. See this beard, he said. It had time to grow here. And he'd been there for five years, waiting for his boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that did not bode well. But my father being my father, we got a tent. He took out his little [INAUDIBLE] and he opened [LAUGHS] his watch [? to ?] make a repair. We didn't have any money. So he started repairing watches for the people in this little camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was pretty scary, because the boat, nobody had heard of it. It was called And it came from Yugoslavia. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have such a bad time. There were all these kids. I was a teenager. The Andrews Sisters were singing 'Rum and Coca Cola.' And we had a tin shed. And I had a couple of boyfriends. And I didn't have to go to school. But there was-- underneath all of that was fear. Because again, you're not allowed to say you're Jewish, they're, they're slitting throats of Jews. I thought it was the Suez Canal but I think it must have been the war of independence in '48.

speaker: interviewee
text: So again, we couldn't say anything. Like this bloody thing, you know? Being Jewish means you're in danger. That was my experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So-- but it was like we didn't know when this-- if they would have told that it will come in a week, in a month, in a year, you knew. But you didn't know. The ship arrived a month later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did it come? Why was it late one month? I mean, you know, you're used to waiting for the train, 10 minutes long. But one month in this, in this-- what was it called, that thing-- El Shatt, El Shatt, near, near Port Said. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so we got on this ship, and I-- Was it that same ship that was promised to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, a little tugboat. Terrible, terrible journey-- we had-- there were some Jewish people in El Shatt that we met, a husband and wife, an old mother-- no, that was another. There were two families. Anyway, this mother was so sick on the boat, I had to change places with her. She was dying, because it was so-- it was such a bumpy ride. And so we got to Melbourne. Well, we got to Fremantle first. And that's when we got to Melbourne.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your first impression from Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I looked down the ship, down the And there were all these sea of white heads and white gloves and white bags. All the women were dressed-- and I thought they were all going to a wedding. But that's how people used to dress in 1948, '49. So I thought they were all very strange.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then somehow, that got confused with the pavlova. You know the cakes? And I thought [LAUGHS] they all looked like they were wearing cakes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, it was, it was really weird times. I came dressed-- you know, after the war, they cut down clothes, and they cut down this suit where they got somewhere for me, a bottle green suit. I mean, with-- you know, talk about power dressing-- great big shoulder pads. And they-- I don't know what those pants are called, but they finish here-- knickerbockers-- and these socks, Scottish patterned, you know, beautiful socks. We were-- I was so proud of these socks. It took-- I don't know where we got them, in Milan or someplace. And I came here, and I was a laughingstock. A girl in a, in a bottle green, cut to size men's suit with Scottish, you know, patterned socks.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school. They put me in Mac. Robertson Girls' High School in year-- in intermediate. And they were learning 'Friends, Romans, countrymen, lend me your ears.' I thought the Australians were crazy. How can you lend ears?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- you know, on the boat or ship, they were teaching me a few English words. So it took me a while till I, till I learned the English language. But there was nothing there for migrants, you know. You didn't have English as a Second Language. You didn't have anything. You just got in there, and you sunk or you swim. So I half sink and half swim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that will finish the tape.
